title: Chapter 21: Home
content: The titanium safe house was impossible.

I don't use that word lightly—impossible—but the space behind that glowing door defied every law of architecture, physics, and real estate that I'd ever known. What had been a utility closet, maybe six feet by four, was now...
"Six thousand square feet," Sarah breathed, stepping through the door ahead of me. "At least."

An open-plan living space that would have made an interior designer weep with envy. Brushed titanium walls with embedded LED strips that cast a warm, amber glow. A kitchen with stainless steel appliances, a walk-in refrigerator already humming with stockpiled food, and a water purifier connected to... somewhere. Bedrooms—actual bedrooms, with real mattresses and clean sheets and windows that looked out onto... a simulation of a sunset, rendered in high-definition on screens that were indistinguishable from glass.
"This is insane," Jessica said, the first full sentence she'd spoken that didn't involve apologizing or crying.
"This is *home*," Luna corrected, and she was right.

The system had built us a fortress. Not just a safe room—a *home*. A space designed for living, not just surviving. Hydroponic gardens in a dedicated bay, their grow-lights casting a green glow over rows of seedlings that would produce actual vegetables within weeks. An armory—locked, for now, with a biometric seal that responded to my fingerprint—that contained weapons I couldn't yet access. A medical bay, fully stocked, with an examination table and cabinets full of supplies.
And in the center of the living space, a circular couch that could seat eight, surrounding a low table made of polished titanium.
"Where does the power come from?" Mia asked, doing a tactical sweep of the perimeter with the practiced eye of someone who checked every corner for threats.

"Geothermal tap," I said, reading the system's schematic that had appeared on my watch. "Independent of the building's grid. Water is recycled and purified through a closed-loop system. Air filtration handles particulates and biological contaminants."
"We could live in here indefinitely," Sarah said, her voice hushed. "For *years*."

"That's the point." I looked around at the impossible space—at the home that the apocalypse had somehow given us—and felt the weight of six people's hopes pressing against my chest. "This is what we were fighting for. Not just survival. A life."

Boss emerged from Sarah's arms—he'd been carried, because he refused to walk on unfamiliar surfaces without a chaperone—and made a beeline for the circular couch, where he claimed the warmest spot with the authority of a creature who'd never doubted his right to the best seat in any room.
"Approved," Luna said solemnly, watching the cat settle in.
That night, we had the first real meal since the outbreak.

Not system-dispensed raw beef or canned beans, but an actual, home-cooked dinner. Sarah, it turned out, could cook—she'd worked part-time at her aunt's restaurant during college, and the safe house's stocked kitchen gave her ingredients she'd only dreamed of. Pasta with canned marinara, garlic bread made from scratch, a salad from the hydroponic garden's first tiny harvest of lettuce, and for dessert, canned peaches served in actual bowls with actual spoons.
We ate at the titanium table, all six of us plus the cat, passing dishes and making sounds that I hadn't heard since before the screaming started.
"God, I missed real food," Rick said, then caught himself, glancing at Maria with guilt.
Maria hadn't touched her pasta. She was staring at it, fork in hand, like she didn't know what to do with food that wasn't being rationed by a captor.
"Eat," Sarah said gently, touching her arm. "It's not a trick. It's not a test. It's just dinner."
Maria took a bite. Then another. Then she was eating the same way Luna ate her beef—with desperate, grateful intensity—and nobody said a word because we all understood.

**QUEST UPDATE: SARAH MITCHELL — ROMANCE QUEST**
**AFFECTION: 42%**
**MILESTONE: Shared vulnerability (group meal in safe space)**
**NOTE: Target is beginning to associate safety with your presence. Continue authentic emotional engagement.**

The system was coaching me again. I ignored it—not because it was wrong, but because right now, Sarah wasn't a quest. She was a person eating pasta for the first time in a week, and the only reward I cared about was the look on her face when she realized she was safe.

After dinner, while the others explored the safe house—Jessica had discovered the entertainment system, which included a library of pre-loaded movies and music; Mia was inventorying the armory's sealed contents; Rick and Maria were having a quiet conversation in one of the bedrooms, beginning the process of two abused people recognizing each other—I found myself alone on the circular couch with Luna.
She was full—system meat, plus she'd stolen a piece of garlic bread when Sarah wasn't looking—and she leaned against me with the boneless contentment of a cat in a sunbeam.
"Good day," she said.
"Yeah. First good day in a while."
"The man—Derek. You let him live."
It wasn't an accusation. Just an observation, offered the way Luna offered everything: directly, without judgment, waiting for my response.
"I couldn't kill him," I said. "Not in cold blood. I'm not... I'm not that person."
"Maybe you should be." She said it softly, without heat. "Some people are dangerous. You let them live, they hurt people again."
"I know. But if we kill everyone who might be dangerous, we become the thing we're fighting against."
Luna considered this. Her fingers traced patterns on my arm—absent, thoughtful, the gesture of a mind working through a problem.

"I was dangerous," she said finally. "I was dead. I could have killed you. You didn't kill me. You fed me. You stayed." She looked up at me, her dark brown eyes shining in the amber light. "Maybe people can change. Maybe I am proof."
My throat tightened. "You're not like Derek, Luna."
"I was a monster. Like him. The food—you—made me not a monster. Maybe food doesn't work for him." She paused. "Maybe nothing works for him. Time will tell."

She was right. Time would tell. Derek was alone on Floor 8, surrounded by zombies, with a week of supplies and a lifetime of bad decisions. Whatever happened to him—redemption or ruin—was out of my hands now.
I'd done what I could. I'd chosen mercy over murder.
I'd have to live with the consequences, whatever they were.
"Hey," Luna said, poking my arm. "Stop thinking. Watch movie."
"Movie?"
She pointed at the entertainment system, where Jessica had cued up a film. The screen showed a title I recognized: *Die Hard*.
"Nakatomi Plaza," Luna said with satisfaction. "Man trapped in building. Fights bad guys. Very relevant."
I laughed. I couldn't help it. Luna—a zombie virologist who was learning about the world through action movies—had just identified the most appropriate film for our situation.
"You're amazing," I told her.
"You said before. Still true." She snuggled closer. "Now watch. Bruce Willis has things to teach us."

We watched the movie. All of us, together, on the circular couch in our titanium fortress, eating leftover garlic bread and laughing at the one-liners and flinching at the explosions. Maria fell asleep on Sarah's shoulder. Jessica quoted every line of dialogue before the characters said it. Mia analyzed the combat tactics with dismissive snorts.
And somewhere in the middle of it all, Sarah's hand found mine on the armrest.
Not a romantic gesture—just a touch. A point of contact. A bridge between two people who'd been alone for too long.
I didn't pull away. Neither did she.
**AFFECTION UPDATE: SARAH MITCHELL — 46%**
Four percent from the halfway mark. Four percent from the moment when the system would start taking her romance quest seriously.
But I wasn't thinking about the system.
I was thinking about the warmth of her hand, and the impossible fact that I wasn't alone anymore, and the movie playing on the screen where a man in a tank top saved everyone against impossible odds.
*Yippee-ki-yay,* I thought.
Maybe we could do this. Maybe we could actually survive.
Maybe we could even live.
